title: UCB — Overview
html:
UCB Account Overview

Governing Thought

UCB is a high-growth, two-franchise biopharmaceutical company (Neurology + Immunology) with €7.7B in revenue and three distinct ZoomRx engagement vectors — Bimzelx immunology (PET across dermatology and rheumatology), Fintepla/Briviact/Nayzilam epilepsy (PET, ATU, hospital tracking), and Rystiggo/Zilbrysq gMG (HCP-Patient Conversations). The account's complexity lies not in volume but in therapeutic diversity: managing UCB means holding context across rare epilepsy (Dravet/LGS), autoimmune dermatology/rheumatology, and neuromuscular disease simultaneously, each with fundamentally different HCP audiences, competitive dynamics, and commercial models. The account is in expansion mode — Bimzelx's rapid indication accumulation (5 approvals in 13 months) and the Neurona Therapeutics acquisition signal a company investing aggressively in both franchises.

The UCB Landscape

At a Glance

Dimension Detail
Company UCB S.A. (Euronext Brussels: UCB.BR)
Headquarters Anderlecht, Brussels, Belgium
CEO Jean-Christophe Tellier (since 2014)
Employees ~10,100
FY 2025 Revenue €7,741M (+26% YoY); Net sales €7,388M (+32%)
Adjusted EBITDA €2,636M (+79%); ~34% margin
Therapeutic Focus Neurology + Immunology (two-franchise model)
ZoomRx Partnership Since ~2022 (earliest SharePoint folder: Briviact Non-User Study)
Active ZoomRx Project Types PET, HCP-Patient Conversations, ATU, Hospital Tracking, Non-User Study
TAs with TA Knowledge 3 full-scope: Immunology, Epilepsy, gMG

Company Context

UCB is a mid-large cap biopharma (~$61B market cap) focused exclusively on neurology and immunology. Unlike diversified pharma companies, UCB's entire commercial engine runs on two franchises — this means every brand decision has outsized portfolio impact.

FY 2025 was a breakout year: Revenue grew 26%, EBITDA nearly doubled, and core EPS more than doubled YoY. This growth is driven by three brands — Bimzelx, Rystiggo, and Fintepla — while legacy Cimzia faces biosimilar erosion.

R&D intensity: UCB invests ~30% of revenue in R&D, among the highest in the industry. This signals strong willingness to invest in evidence generation, market research, and commercial capability building.

Key strategic moves (last 12 months):

  • Neurona Therapeutics acquisition (Apr 2026): $1.15B deal ($650M upfront + $500M milestones) for NRTX-1001, a cell therapy for drug-resistant epilepsy. Expected close Q2 2026.
  • Leadership rotation (Jan 2026): Fiona du Monceau became EVP & CCO (from Patient Evidence); Emmanuel Caeymaex became EVP, Head of Patient Evidence (from CCO). CEO unchanged.
  • 2026 guidance: High-single to low-double digit revenue growth (CER); EBITDA high-single to mid-teens growth.

Portfolio by Therapeutic Area

Immunology — BIMZELX (bimekizumab)

  • What it is: First-in-class dual IL-17A and IL-17F inhibitor — the key differentiator vs. Cosentyx/Taltz (IL-17A only)
  • FY 2025 Revenue: €2,220M+ (largest brand, >28% of total revenue)
  • Approved indications (5 in 13 months):
    1. Moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (Oct 2023)
    2. Psoriatic arthritis (Sep 2024)
    3. Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (Sep 2024)
    4. Ankylosing spondylitis (Sep 2024)
    5. Moderate-to-severe hidradenitis suppurativa (Nov 2024) — first IL-17A/F for HS
  • Key competitors: Cosentyx (secukinumab, Novartis), Taltz (ixekizumab, Lilly), Skyrizi (risankizumab, AbbVie), Tremfya (guselkumab, J&J)
  • Key trials: BE VIVID, BE READY, BE SURE, BE OPTIMAL, BE COMPLETE, BE HEARD
  • ZoomRx work: Bimzelx Dermatology PET (W1 Q1-Q2 2026, active), Bimzelx Rheumatology PET (kick-off phase, active), Bimzelx HCP-Patient Conversations (completed)

Account note: Bimzelx's rapid multi-indication expansion creates compounding project opportunity. Each new indication (HS, PsA, axSpA) introduces a new HCP audience (dermatologists, rheumatologists, surgeons) requiring separate tracking.

Neurology — Epilepsy: BRIVIACT, FINTEPLA, NAYZILAM

  • BRIVIACT (brivaracetam): Selective SV2A ligand for focal (partial-onset) seizures. FY 2025: €758M (+14% YoY). Mature growth brand. Key competitor: XCOPRI (cenobamate, SK Life Science).
  • FINTEPLA (fenfluramine): Serotonin receptor agonist for Dravet syndrome and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome seizures. FY 2025: €427M (+30% YoY). High-growth rare epilepsy brand. Key competitor: Epidiolex (cannabidiol, Jazz). Pipeline: Phase 3 for Rett syndrome (submission planned Q1 2026); 4th epilepsy indication in Phase 3.
  • NAYZILAM (midazolam nasal spray): Rescue therapy for seizure clusters. Revenue not separately disclosed. Key competitor: VALTOCO (diazepam nasal spray).
  • ZoomRx work: Fintepla PET (active, with Qual component), Briviact & Nayzilam PET Studies (completed/stale), Nayzilam & Staccato ATU (Wave 1 complete), Briviact Non-User Study (completed), Briviact Hospital Integrated Study (completed)

Account note: Epilepsy is the deeper engagement in terms of project diversity (PET, ATU, Hospital, Non-User, Qual). Fintepla is the growth engine — Rett syndrome expansion + 4th indication could each generate new tracking asks.

Neurology — gMG: RYSTIGGO, ZILBRYSQ

  • RYSTIGGO (rozanolixizumab): Anti-FcRn monoclonal antibody for generalized myasthenia gravis (AChR+ and MuSK+). FY 2025: €332M (+65% YoY). Key competitor: Vyvgart (efgartigimod, argenx).
  • ZILBRYSQ (zilucoplan): Complement C5 peptide inhibitor (SC) for gMG (AChR+ only). Revenue not separately disclosed (growth driver). Key competitor: Soliris/Ultomiris (eculizumab/ravulizumab, Alexion/AZ).
  • ZoomRx work: gMG HCP-Patient Conversations (completed 2025)
  • Pipeline: Rozanolixizumab Phase 3 for CIDP; Zilucoplan Phase 3 for IMNM

Account note: gMG is UCB's most competitive TA — Vyvgart (argenx) is the dominant new entrant. UCB uniquely offers two distinct MOAs (FcRn + C5), letting physicians choose mechanism based on patient profile. This dual-mechanism positioning is central to any tracking work.

Pipeline to Watch

Asset Mechanism Stage Why It Matters for ZoomRx
Fintepla — Rett Syndrome Serotonin receptor agonist Submission planned Q1 2026 New rare disease indication = new tracking opportunity with different HCP audience (child neurologists)
Fintepla — 4th epilepsy indication Same Phase 3 initiated H1 2026 Expands Fintepla's addressable market and tracking scope
Rozanolixizumab — CIDP Anti-FcRn Phase 3 Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy — new neuromuscular indication, different HCP audience from gMG
Zilucoplan — IMNM Complement C5 Phase 3 Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy — rare muscle disease, potential new tracking
NRTX-1001 (Neurona) Cell therapy Acquired Apr 2026 First cell therapy for drug-resistant epilepsy. Novel modality = new commercial model. Not ZoomRx-relevant near-term.
Bepranemab Anti-tau (Alzheimer's) FDA Fast Track (Feb 2026) Long-horizon but signals UCB's neuroscience ambition beyond epilepsy/MG

ZoomRx Service Footprint

Project Type Immunology (Bimzelx) Epilepsy (Briviact/Fintepla/Nayzilam) gMG (Rystiggo/Zilbrysq)
PET / SFEA X (Derm + Rheum) X (Fintepla, Briviact+Nayzilam) —
HCP-Patient Conversations X (completed) — X (completed)
ATU — X (Nayzilam+Staccato) —
Hospital Tracking — X (Briviact) —
Non-User Study — X (Briviact) —
Qual Research — X (Fintepla PET Qual) —
OCE Tracking — — —
MSL / MEE — — —
FRM Tracking — — —
KAM Tracking — — —
Message Testing — — —
Digital Tracking — — —
Commercial Analytics / SFTO — — —
Launch KPI Tracking — — —
Ferma Congress — — —
Social Listening — — —

Key observation: The engagement footprint is narrow but growing. PET and HCP-Pt are the established project types. The bottom half of this matrix is entirely open — MSL/MEE, FRM, KAM, Message Testing, Digital, and Ferma Congress represent the expansion opportunity.

Competitive Landscape Summary

Brand Primary Competitor Competitive Dynamic Implication for ZoomRx Work
BIMZELX Skyrizi (AbbVie), Cosentyx (Novartis) Dual IL-17A/F vs. IL-23 (Skyrizi) vs. IL-17A only (Cosentyx). Bimzelx offers fastest clearance but must overcome safety monitoring concerns (oral candidiasis, IBD warning) PET tracks SOV and message effectiveness in derm/rheum; clearance speed vs. safety profile is the core messaging tension
BRIVIACT XCOPRI (cenobamate, SK Life Science) SV2A ligand vs. dual-MOA newcomer in adjunctive focal seizures. XCOPRI is the fastest-growing ASM, aggressively marketed PET measures competitive position; Briviact's tolerability advantage vs. XCOPRI's efficacy narrative is the central rep battle
FINTEPLA Epidiolex (cannabidiol, Jazz) Both approved in Dravet + LGS. Fintepla growing faster (+30% vs. Epidiolex trajectory). REMS requirement (echocardiogram monitoring) creates access friction PET in rare epilepsy has small HCP universe; competitive narrative is efficacy vs. monitoring burden
RYSTIGGO Vyvgart (efgartigimod, argenx) Both FcRn inhibitors. Vyvgart is the clear market leader (~$2B+). Rystiggo differentiates on MuSK+ coverage and dosing convenience HCP-Pt conversations reveal treatment decision drivers; FcRn class competition is about physician comfort and real-world tolerability
ZILBRYSQ Soliris/Ultomiris (Alexion/AZ) SC complement C5 vs. IV complement C5. Zilbrysq's SC convenience is the primary differentiator over established IV competitors Complements Rystiggo positioning — UCB can offer mechanism choice (FcRn vs. C5) based on patient profile

Back to Accounts Hub | Home
